mapping.a_element: inviscid, rotational transonic accretion flow as the background fluid
mapping.b_element: 2+1 dimensional rotating draining bathtub fluid flow as the background

mapping.a_element: linear time-dependent perturbation governed by the perturbation equation
mapping.b_element: small perturbations whose quasinormal modes are computed

mapping.a_element: acoustic geometry / effective acoustic metric emerging from the perturbation equation
mapping.b_element: acoustic black hole metric (closest analogue to Kerr)

mapping.a_element: angular momentum / rotation of the accretion flow
mapping.b_element: rotation parameter B of the draining bathtub

mapping.a_element: transonic (sonic/critical) point acting as an acoustic horizon
mapping.b_element: acoustic horizon of the rotating acoustic black hole

mapping.a_element: flow shown stable under adiabatic, radially propagating perturbations (no growth)
mapping.b_element: flow shown stable: no sign change in the imaginary part of the QN frequencies (no growing modes)

brief_justification: Both realize analogue-gravity machinery: linear acoustic perturbations of a rotating transonic fluid flow are governed by a wave equation on an emergent effective 'acoustic' (black-hole) geometry, and stability is decided by whether those perturbations grow. Paper A gets the acoustic geometry and stability analytically from a pseudo-potential-invariant perturbation equation, while paper B extracts them numerically via quasinormal-mode frequencies, but the governing structure (acoustic metric from fluid perturbations + linear stability of the acoustic horizon) is the same.
